destination.name: Veneto
destination.tagline: Amarone hills, Prosecco ridges, the full arc of Italian wine
destination.overview: Italy's most prolific DOC wine region by volume and one of its most prestigious by pedigree. From the dried-grape Amarone DOCG of Valpolicella to the UNESCO-listed Prosecco Superiore hills of Conegliano Valdobbiadene, Soave's volcanic Garganega and Lake Garda's Lugana, the Veneto spans 11 DOCGs and 29 DOCs across Verona, Treviso, Vicenza and Padova provinces.
destination.blurb: Italy's most prolific and diverse wine region, from Valpolicella's Amarone DOCG and Soave Classico to the UNESCO Prosecco Superiore hills of Conegliano Valdobbiadene and Lake Garda's Lugana.
destination.hero_image_credit: Photo by Damiano Baschiera on Unsplash
destination.hero_image_alt: Veneto vineyard terraces on the UNESCO Prosecco hills above Valdobbiadene

index.title: Veneto Wine Region Guide | Cork & Curve
index.description: Explore Veneto wine estates, sub-appellations and iconic cuvees from Amarone della Valpolicella DOCG and Soave Classico to Prosecco Superiore DOCG and Lugana DOC.

vineyards.description: Discover Veneto wine estates from Quintarelli and Dal Forno Romano to Pieropan, Nino Franco and Maculan. Tasting programmes and production details for 50+ producers.

wines.title: Veneto Wines Catalog | Cork & Curve
wines.description: Browse Veneto cuvees from Amarone and Valpolicella Ripasso to Soave Superiore DOCG, Prosecco Superiore and Lugana Riserva. Taste profiles, pairings and scores.

tasting-rooms.title: Veneto Wine Tasting Rooms | Cork & Curve
tasting-rooms.description: Best Veneto tasting rooms and cellar doors from Valpolicella Classico estates and Soave Classico hilltop producers to Prosecco DOCG houses in Valdobbiadene.

wine-bars.title: Veneto Wine Bars and Enoteche | Cork & Curve
wine-bars.description: Sommelier-driven wine bars and enoteche in Verona, Venice and Treviso pouring Amarone, Soave Superiore, Prosecco Superiore and Lugana by the glass.

wine-restaurants.title: Veneto Wine Restaurants | Cork & Curve
wine-restaurants.description: Veneto restaurants with serious wine programs, from Verona trattorias pouring Amarone with braised beef to Treviso osterie pairing Prosecco with cicchetti.

signature-wines.title: Iconic Veneto Wines | Cork & Curve
signature-wines.description: The iconic bottles that define the Veneto, from Quintarelli Amarone and Pieropan La Rocca to Nino Franco Grave di Stecca and Maculan Torcolato.

signature-grapes.title: Veneto Signature Grapes | Cork & Curve
signature-grapes.description: Veneto grape varieties from Corvina, Corvinone and Garganega in the Verona hills to Glera in the Prosecco ridges, Turbiana at Lake Garda and Vespaiola in Breganze.

neighborhoods.title: Veneto Sub-Appellations and Wine Areas | Cork & Curve
neighborhoods.description: Veneto wine sub-appellations from Valpolicella Classico and Soave Classico to the UNESCO Conegliano Valdobbiadene hills, Asolo, Lugana and Colli Euganei.

faqs.question: What is Amarone della Valpolicella?
faqs.answer: Amarone della Valpolicella DOCG is a dry red wine made from partially dried (appassimento) Corvina, Corvinone and Rondinella grapes in the Verona hills. Drying for 90-120 days concentrates sugars which are fermented to dryness, yielding 15-17% ABV wines with extraordinary depth and 20-40 year ageing potential. The DOCG was established in 2009, separating Amarone from Valpolicella DOC.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Amarone and Ripasso?
faqs.answer: Ripasso della Valpolicella DOC is a lighter, more accessible style made by refermented Valpolicella DOC wine over the leftover grape skins from Amarone production. The refermentation adds body, colour and Amarone-like richness at a much lower price point, typically 13-14% ABV vs Amarone's 15-17%.

faqs.question: What grapes make Soave?
faqs.answer: Soave DOC must contain at least 70% Garganega, often blended with Trebbiano di Soave (also called Verdicchio). The best Soave Classico and Soave Superiore DOCG comes from volcanic hillsides between the Soave castle and Monteforte d'Alpone where basalt and tufa soils give the Garganega exceptional mineral intensity.

faqs.question: What is the Prosecco Superiore DOCG?
faqs.answer: Conegliano Valdobbiadene Prosecco Superiore DOCG covers the UNESCO-listed hillsides between Conegliano and Valdobbiadene in Treviso province. Made from Glera using the Charmat (tank) method, it is a richer, more complex sparkling wine than the broader Prosecco DOC. The Cartizze single-hill grand cru and single-commune Rive wines represent the appellation's highest tier.

faqs.question: When is the best time to visit Veneto wine estates?
faqs.answer: Late September through mid-October is harvest season (vendemmia) in both Valpolicella and Soave Classico, with the Glera harvest in Valdobbiadene typically in late September. Many estates welcome visitors during harvest. For Amarone appassimento visits (seeing the drying lofts), October-November is ideal. Spring (April-May) and early autumn are the best for comfortable travel and full estate programmes.

faqs.question: Which Veneto wines are best for ageing?
faqs.answer: Amarone della Valpolicella DOCG is the region's great ageing wine, with top producers' examples drinking best 10-30 years from vintage. Recioto della Valpolicella DOCG (sweet, from the same grapes) also ages magnificently. Single-vineyard Soave Superiore DOCG from Pieropan, Gini and Prà can develop impressively over 5-15 years. Lugana DOC Riserva is emerging as an age-worthy white.
